summary: This page is part of a wire welding troubleshooting guide, covering four common weld bead defects: Porosity, Excessive Spatter, Crooked/Wavy Bead, and Burn-Through. Each section includes a top-view diagram of the defect and a numbered list of possible causes and corresponding solutions.
text:
Wire Weld – Porosity
Small cavities or holes in the bead.
TOP VIEW [diagram showing weld bead with small holes/cavities]
POSSIBLE CAUSES AND SOLUTIONS
1. Incorrect polarity: Check that polarity is set correctly for type of welding.
2. Insufficient shielding gas (MIG only): Increase flow of gas. Clean nozzle. Maintain proper CTWD.
3. Incorrect shielding gas (MIG only): Use shielding gas recommended by wire supplier.
4. Dirty workpiece or welding wire: Clean workpiece down to bare metal. Make certain that wire is clean and free from oil, coatings, and other residues.
5. Inconsistent travel speed: Maintain steady travel speed.
6. CTWD too long: Reduce CTWD.

Wire Weld – Excessive Spatter
Fine spatter is normal. Spatter that is grainy and large is a problem.
TOP VIEW [diagram showing weld bead with spatter particles]
POSSIBLE CAUSES AND SOLUTIONS
1. Dirty workpiece or welding wire: Clean workpiece down to bare metal. Make certain that wire is clean and free from oil, coatings, and other residues.
2. Incorrect polarity: Check that polarity is set correctly for type of welding.
3. Insufficient shielding gas (MIG only): Increase flow of gas. Clean nozzle. Maintain proper CTWD.
4. Wire feeding too fast: Reduce wire feed speed.
5. CTWD too long: Reduce CTWD.

Wire Weld – Crooked/Wavy Bead
TOP VIEW [diagram showing wavy, uneven weld bead]
POSSIBLE CAUSES AND SOLUTIONS
1. Inaccurate welding: Use two hands or rest hand on steady surface.
2. Inconsistent travel speed: Maintain steady travel speed.
3. CTWD too long: Reduce CTWD.

Wire Weld – Burn-Through
Base material melts away, leaving a hole in the weld.
TOP VIEW [diagram showing weld bead with a hole/gap from burn-through]
POSSIBLE CAUSES AND SOLUTIONS
1. Workpiece overheating: Reduce current and/or wire feed speed.
2. Travel speed too slow: Increase travel speed and ensure that travel speed is kept steady.
3. Excessive material at weld: Reduce wire feed speed.

Item 57812
For technical questions, please call 1-800-444-3353.
Page 37

figures.caption: Wire Weld – Porosity, Top View
figures.description: Top view diagram of a weld bead showing small cavities or holes scattered throughout the bead, illustrating porosity defect.

figures.caption: Wire Weld – Excessive Spatter, Top View
figures.description: Top view diagram of a weld bead surrounded by scattered spatter particles, illustrating grainy, large spatter as a defect while fine spatter is normal.

figures.caption: Wire Weld – Crooked/Wavy Bead, Top View
figures.description: Top view diagram of a weld bead with an uneven, wavy, crooked pattern instead of a straight consistent bead.

key_facts: Porosity defect appears as small cavities or holes in the weld bead.

Porosity causes include: incorrect polarity, insufficient shielding gas (MIG only), incorrect shielding gas (MIG only), dirty workpiece or wire, inconsistent travel speed, and CTWD (Contact Tip to Work Distance) too long.
Fine spatter during welding is normal; grainy and large spatter indicates a problem.
Excessive spatter causes include: dirty workpiece or welding wire, incorrect polarity, insufficient shielding gas (MIG only), wire feeding too fast, and CTWD too long.
Crooked/wavy bead causes include inaccurate welding technique, inconsistent travel speed, and CTWD too long.
Recommended fix for inaccurate/crooked welding: use two hands or rest hand on a steady surface.
Burn-through occurs when base material melts away, leaving a hole in the weld.
Burn-through causes include workpiece overheating, travel speed too slow, and excessive material at weld (wire feed speed too high).
To fix burn-through: reduce current and/or wire feed speed, increase and steady travel speed, or reduce wire feed speed.
CTWD stands for Contact Tip to Work Distance; maintaining proper CTWD or reducing it is a common fix for multiple wire welding defects.
